content.text: A GenAI roadmap that lists capabilities without sequencing their dependencies is a wish list. Dependency sequencing answers a harder question: given all the capabilities we want to build, in what order must they be built so that each capability has what it needs to succeed? Horizon planning answers the complementary question: how far out should we plan in detail, and how do we handle the fact that GenAI technology and enterprise requirements are both moving faster than a traditional 3-year plan can accommodate?

attrs.alt: Three-horizon GenAI roadmap with dependency arrows

content.text: The Three-Horizon Model for GenAI

McKinsey's three-horizon framework maps well to GenAI programme planning because the technology and its enterprise applications are evolving on different time constants. The framework avoids the trap of treating all capabilities as equally uncertain — some things (LLM gateway, risk framework) are well-understood and can be planned in detail; others (cross-BU AI assistants, fine-tuned domain models) depend on capabilities that do not yet exist in the enterprise.
Horizon
Time Range
Planning Mode
Certainty Level
Characteristic Work
H1: Now
0–9 months
Detailed execution plan with milestone dates
High — dependencies are known; technology is proven
Platform foundation, governance framework, first pilots
H2: Next
6–18 months (overlaps H1)
Shape-level planning — what and approximate when, not detailed how
Medium — depends on H1 outcomes; technology mostly stable
Scale infrastructure, BU enablement, self-serve intake
H3: Future
15+ months (overlaps H2)
Directional only — strategic intent, no milestone commitments
Low — depends on H2 outcomes; technology still evolving
Fine-tuning, advanced agents, enterprise AI assistant

The overlap between horizons is intentional. H2 planning begins while H1 is still executing — the team uses early H1 feedback to sharpen H2 scope. Similarly, H3 direction is set during H2 based on what the programme learns about BU demand and platform maturity. A programme that waits to plan H2 until H1 is complete will lose 6–9 months of sequencing lead time.

Rule of thumb: plan H1 to the sprint, H2 to the quarter, H3 to the half-year. Any finer granularity in H2 or H3 is false precision that will need to be discarded when H1 outcomes shape what is actually feasible.
Dependency Types in GenAI Roadmaps

GenAI programme dependencies fall into four types. Misidentifying the type leads to incorrect sequencing — the most common mistake is treating a soft dependency (nice to have before) as a hard dependency (must have before), which causes unnecessary serialisation of work that could run in parallel.
Dependency Type
Definition
Example
Sequencing Implication
Hard technical dependency
Capability B literally cannot function without Capability A
RAG pipeline cannot run without a vector store; self-serve intake cannot route without a risk tier classifier
A must be complete before B can start — no workaround
Soft quality dependency
Capability B works without A, but produces worse results or higher risk
Pilots can run without guardrail middleware, but with higher hallucination risk to end users
A should precede B but can overlap; risk of running B without A must be explicitly accepted
Resource dependency
Capability A and B need the same person, team, or budget allocation and cannot run in parallel
The single platform engineer cannot build the model registry and the embedding service simultaneously
Sequence by priority; identify whether the resource constraint can be resolved by hiring or contracting
Governance dependency
Capability B requires sign-off, approval, or data access that itself takes time
Customer-facing GenAI features require DPA amendment and legal sign-off (8–12 weeks typical)
Start the governance track in parallel with technical build; never discover this dependency when B is ready to deploy
Building the Dependency Graph

The dependency graph is the structural artefact that drives roadmap sequencing. It is not a Gantt chart (which shows time) but a directed acyclic graph (DAG) where nodes are capabilities and edges are dependencies. The critical path through the DAG determines the minimum programme duration.

# GenAI programme dependency graph and critical path analysis
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from collections import defaultdict, deque

@dataclass
class Capability:
    id: str
    name: str
    horizon: int               # 1, 2, or 3
    duration_weeks: int
    dependencies: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)   # capability ids

# Representative GenAI programme capabilities
CAPABILITIES = [
    Capability("llm-gateway",      "LLM Gateway + Auth",            1, 6,  []),
    Capability("coe-formed",       "CoE Team (3 FTE)",               1, 8,  []),
    Capability("risk-framework",   "Risk Framework v1",              1, 4,  ["coe-formed"]),
    Capability("pilot-1",          "Pilot Use Case #1",              1, 8,  ["llm-gateway", "risk-framework"]),
    Capability("model-registry",   "Model Registry",                 1, 4,  ["llm-gateway"]),
    Capability("guardrails",       "Guardrail Middleware",           2, 6,  ["llm-gateway", "risk-framework"]),
    Capability("self-serve",       "Self-Serve Intake (Tier 1)",     2, 8,  ["risk-framework", "coe-formed"]),
    Capability("rag-pipeline",     "Shared RAG Pipeline",            2, 10, ["llm-gateway", "model-registry"]),
    Capability("guild-network",    "BU Guild Network",               2, 12, ["coe-formed", "self-serve"]),
    Capability("chargeback",       "Chargeback / Cost Allocation",   2, 6,  ["coe-formed"]),
    Capability("fine-tuning",      "LoRA Fine-Tuning Pipeline",      3, 16, ["model-registry", "rag-pipeline"]),
    Capability("agent-platform",   "Agentic Workflow Platform",      3, 20, ["guardrails", "rag-pipeline"]),
    Capability("model-router",     "Model Tier Router",              3, 8,  ["llm-gateway", "fine-tuning"]),
]

def compute_earliest_start(caps: list[Capability]) -> dict[str, int]:
    cap_map = {c.id: c for c in caps}
    earliest = {}
    in_degree = defaultdict(int)
    rev = defaultdict(list)  # successor lists

    for c in caps:
        for dep in c.dependencies:
            in_degree[c.id] += 1
            rev[dep].append(c.id)

    queue = deque(c.id for c in caps if in_degree[c.id] == 0)
    for cid in queue:
        earliest[cid] = 0

    while queue:
        cid = queue.popleft()
        cap = cap_map[cid]
        finish = earliest[cid] + cap.duration_weeks
        for succ in rev[cid]:
            earliest[succ] = max(earliest.get(succ, 0), finish)
            in_degree[succ] -= 1
            if in_degree[succ] == 0:
                queue.append(succ)

    return earliest

earliest = compute_earliest_start(CAPABILITIES)
cap_map = {c.id: c for c in CAPABILITIES}

# Find critical path (latest finish time)
critical_finish = max(earliest[c.id] + c.duration_weeks for c in CAPABILITIES)
print(f"Minimum programme duration: {critical_finish} weeks ({critical_finish/4:.1f} months)")

print("\nH1 capabilities and their earliest start week:")
for c in CAPABILITIES:
    if c.horizon == 1:
        finish = earliest[c.id] + c.duration_weeks
        print(f"  Week {earliest[c.id]:>2}–{finish:>2}: {c.name}")
# Minimum programme duration: 38 weeks (9.5 months)
# H1 capabilities and their earliest start week:
#   Week  0– 6: LLM Gateway + Auth
#   Week  0– 8: CoE Team (3 FTE)
#   Week  8–12: Risk Framework v1
#   Week  0– 4: (various H1 starts...)
#   Week 12–20: Pilot Use Case #1
Reading the dependency graph
Three patterns in the dependency graph require active management:
Critical path items

— Capabilities on the longest path through the DAG. A delay to any critical path item delays the entire programme by the same amount. The LLM gateway is almost always on the critical path because it is a direct dependency of both pilots and guardrails.
Convergence points

— Nodes with many incoming dependency edges. These are high-risk items because they require multiple predecessor capabilities to be ready simultaneously. The self-serve intake (depends on risk framework + CoE formation + intake portal) is a typical convergence point.
Parallelisable tracks
— Subgraphs with no shared dependencies. In the example above, CoE formation and LLM gateway can run in parallel — the programme manager should staff and start them simultaneously, not sequentially.
Horizon Planning in Practice
H1 planning: detailed execution

H1 planning produces a sprint-level execution plan with named owners, weekly milestones, and defined done criteria for each capability. The dependency graph is the input; the output is a sequenced backlog the CoE engineering team works from.
H1 Planning Element
Detail Required
Owner
Capability backlog
Every H1 capability broken into engineering tasks; each task ≤ 1 sprint
Platform Lead
Done criteria
Explicit, testable definition of done for each capability (not 'built' but 'deployed to production with SLA documented')
CoE Lead + Platform Lead
Resource allocation
Which engineer or team builds each capability; no over-allocation
CoE Lead
Dependency schedule
For each hard dependency, the earliest date the successor can start given predecessor timelines
Programme Manager
Risk log
For each H1 capability, the top risk and its owner
CoE Lead
H2 planning: shape-level

H2 planning does not produce sprint-level detail — it produces a capability list with rough sizing, sequencing constraints relative to H1, and a quarterly slot for each capability. The key H2 planning question is: what must be true at the end of H1 for each H2 capability to be startable?

# H2 shape-level planning: entry criteria for H2 capabilities
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class H2Capability:
    name: str
    estimated_duration_weeks: int   # rough; will be refined as H1 closes
    h1_entry_criteria: list[str]    # what H1 must deliver for this to be startable
    target_quarter: str             # approximate slot

H2_CAPABILITIES = [
    H2Capability(
        name="Guardrail Middleware",
        estimated_duration_weeks=6,
        h1_entry_criteria=[
            "LLM gateway deployed and stable (99.5% uptime for 4 weeks)",
            "Risk framework v1 published and approved by Legal",
            "At least one pilot live to validate guardrail requirements",
        ],
        target_quarter="Q2",
    ),
    H2Capability(
        name="Self-Serve Intake (Tier 1)",
        estimated_duration_weeks=8,
        h1_entry_criteria=[
            "Risk tier classification rules documented and validated",
            "CoE intake process manual version operational for ≥ 60 days",
            "At least 10 intake requests processed manually (to validate policy rules)",
        ],
        target_quarter="Q2",
    ),
    H2Capability(
        name="Shared RAG Pipeline",
        estimated_duration_weeks=10,
        h1_entry_criteria=[
            "LLM gateway stable",
            "Model registry read-only version deployed",
            "At least one BU use case requiring retrieval identified and scoped",
        ],
        target_quarter="Q3",
    ),
]

for cap in H2_CAPABILITIES:
    print(f"\n{cap.name} [{cap.target_quarter}, ~{cap.estimated_duration_weeks}w]")
    for criterion in cap.h1_entry_criteria:
        print(f"  ✓ {criterion}")
H3 planning: directional only

H3 items should be written as strategic intent, not project plans. The distinction matters: a strategic intent statement ('we intend to provide a LoRA fine-tuning capability for BUs with domain-specific accuracy requirements') gives the programme direction and allows early investments (GPU procurement, training data governance policy) without committing to a delivery date that will almost certainly change.

H3 items are reviewed quarterly at the steering committee and promoted to H2 when: the H2 predecessor capabilities are confirmed on track; the business case is quantified; the technology is sufficiently mature; and a delivery team can be named.
Common Sequencing Mistakes
Mistake
What Goes Wrong
Correct Approach
Placing user-facing features in H1 before governance is ready
Customer-facing GenAI deployed without PII guardrails or legal sign-off; incident creates regulatory exposure before programme has any wins to offset it
All user-facing H1 capabilities require the risk framework to be complete; governance track must run in parallel with technical build, not after
Serialising parallel tracks
Platform engineering waits for CoE formation to complete before starting gateway; adds 8 weeks to H1 critical path unnecessarily
Identify which H1 capabilities have no dependencies and start them in week 1 simultaneously
Fine-tuning in H1 or early H2
Fine-tuning requires training data curation, GPU compute procurement, and eval pipeline — none of which exist in H1; teams waste months on premature complexity
Fine-tuning belongs in H3 for most programmes; prompt engineering + RAG covers 90% of H1/H2 use cases more cheaply
No dependency on pilot learnings
H2 capabilities planned in detail before H1 pilots provide feedback; self-serve intake designed around assumed requirements that pilots reveal are wrong
H2 detailed planning (sprint-level) should not begin until H1 pilot retrospective is complete
Over-planning H3

Programme produces a detailed 3-year roadmap for H3 capabilities; when the technology landscape shifts (new model capability, new compliance requirement), the plan is obsolete but politically hard to change
H3 is directional only; never produce sprint-level H3 plans more than 6 months in advance
Managing Roadmap Uncertainty

The GenAI technology landscape changes faster than most enterprise planning cycles. Two capabilities that were H3 in January may be available as managed services by June, changing both the feasibility and the required investment. The roadmap needs a lightweight update mechanism that does not require a full steering committee reset.
Quarterly roadmap review cadence
Horizon shift review

— For each H2 and H3 item, assess: has any predecessor moved significantly? Has the technology changed (model capability improvement, new managed service) that changes effort estimate? Has BU demand changed? Items that have shifted are updated in the roadmap before the next quarter starts.
H3 promotion review

— Which H3 items are ready to be promoted to H2? Promotion requires: H2 entry criteria can be written; a delivery team can be named; rough effort estimate can be produced; business case can be quantified.
H1 backlog replenishment
— As H1 capabilities complete, the sprint backlog pulls from the H2 queue. The programme manager confirms dependencies are met before pulling each item.

# Quarterly roadmap review: automated dependency check
from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date

@dataclass
class CapabilityStatus:
    id: str
    name: str
    horizon: int
    status: str         # "not_started" | "in_progress" | "complete"
    completion_date: date | None = None
    dependencies: list[str] = None

    def __post_init__(self):
        if self.dependencies is None:
            self.dependencies = []

def check_h2_readiness(
    capabilities: list[CapabilityStatus],
    as_of: date,
) -> dict[str, list[str]]:
    # Return H2 items with their unmet H1 dependencies.
    cap_map = {c.id: c for c in capabilities}
    h2_items = [c for c in capabilities if c.horizon == 2]
    unmet = {}

    for h2 in h2_items:
        blocking = []
        for dep_id in h2.dependencies:
            dep = cap_map.get(dep_id)
            if dep and dep.status != "complete":
                blocking.append(f"{dep.name} ({dep.status})")
        if blocking:
            unmet[h2.name] = blocking

    return unmet

# Example state check
capabilities = [
    CapabilityStatus("llm-gateway",    "LLM Gateway",      1, "complete", date(2025, 3, 15)),
    CapabilityStatus("risk-framework", "Risk Framework",   1, "complete", date(2025, 4, 1)),
    CapabilityStatus("coe-formed",     "CoE Team",         1, "complete", date(2025, 3, 1)),
    CapabilityStatus("pilot-1",        "Pilot #1",         1, "in_progress", None, ["llm-gateway", "risk-framework"]),
    CapabilityStatus("model-registry", "Model Registry",   1, "in_progress", None, ["llm-gateway"]),
    CapabilityStatus("guardrails",     "Guardrails",       2, "not_started", None, ["llm-gateway", "risk-framework"]),
    CapabilityStatus("self-serve",     "Self-Serve Intake",2, "not_started", None, ["risk-framework", "coe-formed"]),
    CapabilityStatus("rag-pipeline",   "RAG Pipeline",     2, "not_started", None, ["llm-gateway", "model-registry"]),
]

unmet = check_h2_readiness(capabilities, date(2025, 5, 1))
print("H2 capabilities with unmet dependencies:")
for cap_name, blockers in unmet.items():
    print(f"  {cap_name}: blocked by → {', '.join(blockers)}")
print("\nH2 capabilities ready to start:")
for c in capabilities:
    if c.horizon == 2 and c.name not in unmet:
        print(f"  {c.name} — all dependencies complete")
# H2 capabilities with unmet dependencies:
#   RAG Pipeline: blocked by → Model Registry (in_progress)
# H2 capabilities ready to start:
#   Guardrails — all dependencies complete
#   Self-Serve Intake — all dependencies complete
Communicating the Roadmap to Stakeholders
Different stakeholders need different views of the same roadmap. The dependency graph is an engineering artefact — it is not what you show the steering committee or BU leaders.
Audience
What They Need to See
Format
Steering committee
Which capabilities land in which quarter; what business outcomes each enables; phase gate dates and criteria
Horizon swimlane view (as in the diagram) with outcome labels, not technical names
CFO / Finance
Investment by phase; which capabilities unlock the modelled ROI; no H3 investment without H2 gate approval
Cost waterfall aligned to horizon timeline; gate criteria tied to budget release
BU leaders
When will capability X be available for my BU? What do I need to do to be ready (data preparation, staff training)?
BU-specific view: capabilities that enable BU use cases, with BU readiness tasks called out
CoE platform team
Dependency graph; sprint assignments; critical path; resource allocation
Detailed dependency DAG; sprint backlog; RAID log
Enterprise architecture
Integration points; data flow; compliance checkpoints; vendor dependencies
Architecture decision records (ADRs); integration diagram
Summary
Three-horizon planning (H1: now, H2: next, H3: future) matches GenAI uncertainty: H1 is detailed execution, H2 is shape-level planning, H3 is directional intent only
Dependency types matter: hard technical dependencies force sequencing; soft quality dependencies can overlap; resource and governance dependencies are often the actual bottleneck
Build the dependency DAG before building the Gantt — critical path analysis shows the minimum programme duration and which capabilities can run in parallel
H2 detailed planning (sprint-level) should not begin until H1 pilot retrospective confirms requirements; never produce sprint-level H3 plans more than 6 months in advance
Quarterly roadmap reviews check H2 dependency readiness, promote H3 items that are ready, and replenish the H1 sprint backlog from the H2 queue
Different audiences need different roadmap views: steering committee sees horizon swimlanes; BU leaders see when their capabilities land; engineering sees the dependency graph
